choices.prompt: According to the module, what is a lifecycle management policy?
choices: A JSON document attached to the storage account, containing rules with filters and age-based actions that the platform enforces continuously
A nightly script you write and schedule to walk every blob and decide which to move or delete
A compute job you must run and monitor that paginates through millions of objects
An immutability setting that prevents any blob from being modified or deleted for a fixed period
correct_answers: A JSON document attached to the storage account, containing rules with filters and age-based actions that the platform enforces continuously

choices.prompt: You save a new lifecycle rule that archives blobs after 90 days, and a few minutes later the tier is unchanged. Based on the module, what is the correct interpretation?
choices: The policy is not broken; lifecycle evaluation runs periodically (roughly daily) and can take up to about a day to first run, acting on the next pass after the threshold
The policy is broken because a correctly applied rule tiers blobs within seconds of crossing the age threshold
The rule's filter is wrong because archive actions only apply to page blobs, not block blobs
Soft delete must be disabled before archive actions can take effect, so the rule is being blocked
correct_answers: The policy is not broken; lifecycle evaluation runs periodically (roughly daily) and can take up to about a day to first run, acting on the next pass after the threshold

choices.prompt: In the Meridian walkthrough, why does the module run the command that enables 14-day soft delete and versioning BEFORE applying the lifecycle policy?
choices: So that if the prefix or a threshold is wrong, blobs deleted by the policy are recoverable rather than gone, since enabling protection after the fact does not protect data already purged
Because lifecycle policies cannot be created on a storage account until soft delete and versioning are both enabled
Because soft delete and versioning are required to make the daysAfterModificationGreaterThan age clock start counting
Because versioning automatically moves overwritten blobs to the archive tier, satisfying the retention requirement
correct_answers: So that if the prefix or a threshold is wrong, blobs deleted by the policy are recoverable rather than gone, since enabling protection after the fact does not protect data already purged

choices.prompt: The module contrasts lifecycle expiry with immutability policies. Which statements correctly reflect that contrast? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Lifecycle deletes blobs on a schedule but does not prevent early deletion
An immutability policy (time-based retention or legal hold) prevents modification or deletion for a fixed period and is appropriate when retention is a legal requirement
Lifecycle policies are the correct tool to enforce a legally mandated retention period because they block early deletion
Immutability policies tier blobs from hot to cool to archive automatically as they age
correct_answers: Lifecycle deletes blobs on a schedule but does not prevent early deletion
An immutability policy (time-based retention or legal hold) prevents modification or deletion for a fixed period and is appropriate when retention is a legal requirement

choices.prompt: A rule intended only for the proof-of-delivery/ prefix omitted its prefixMatch filter and deletes after 2,555 days. Per the module, what is the risk and the fix?
choices: The rule applies account-wide and will eventually delete blobs in every container; the fix is to add the prefixMatch ["proof-of-delivery/"] filter (and the blockBlob type filter) to scope it
The rule will fail to run at all because a rule with no prefixMatch is invalid JSON and is rejected on apply
The rule will only delete blobs that have last-access tracking enabled, so the fix is to disable that account setting
The risk is that archived blobs become unreadable; the fix is to remove the delete action and rely on versioning instead
correct_answers: The rule applies account-wide and will eventually delete blobs in every container; the fix is to add the prefixMatch ["proof-of-delivery/"] filter (and the blockBlob type filter) to scope it

choices.prompt: According to the module, what does a single rule inside a lifecycle management policy consist of?
choices: A filter selecting which blobs the rule applies to, plus a set of actions keyed to age that move a blob to cool, move it to archive, or delete it
A schedule expression plus a script body that the platform compiles and runs as a nightly cleanup job
A retention period and a legal-hold flag that together prevent the blob from being modified or deleted
A source container and a destination container between which blobs are continuously replicated
correct_answers: A filter selecting which blobs the rule applies to, plus a set of actions keyed to age that move a blob to cool, move it to archive, or delete it

choices.prompt: Per the module, on what can a rule's filter scope which blobs it applies to? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A name prefix (prefixMatch)
Blob type (such as block blobs)
Index tags on the blobs
The geographic region of the storage account

choices.prompt: The module warns about 'archiving data you still read.' What concrete consequence does it describe for a rule that archives blobs the application still requests?
choices: Fast reads turn into multi-hour rehydration, so you should tier to archive only what is genuinely cold
The archive action is silently rejected because archived blobs cannot be requested at all
The blobs are permanently deleted instead of archived once an application reads them
Soft delete is bypassed for archived blobs, making the reads irrecoverable
correct_answers: Fast reads turn into multi-hour rehydration, so you should tier to archive only what is genuinely cold

choices.prompt: How does the module distinguish what blob soft delete protects against versus what blob versioning protects against?
choices: Soft delete retains deleted blobs for a configurable retention period so a delete can be undone within the window; versioning automatically keeps a previous version whenever a blob is overwritten or deleted so the prior state can be restored
Soft delete keeps a copy of every overwrite while versioning only applies to outright deletions performed by a policy rule
Soft delete prevents modification or deletion for a fixed legal period while versioning tiers older versions to archive automatically
Soft delete starts the age clock for lifecycle actions while versioning stops it until a restore is performed
correct_answers: Soft delete retains deleted blobs for a configurable retention period so a delete can be undone within the window; versioning automatically keeps a previous version whenever a blob is overwritten or deleted so the prior state can be restored

choices.prompt: The module says the age clock for lifecycle conditions is driven by blob timestamps. Which timestamps does it name as possible drivers, and what does it note about using last access? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Creation, last modified, or last accessed can drive the age clock depending on the condition you choose
Enabling last-access tracking has its own setting you turn on at the account level
The age clock is always driven solely by the blob's creation time and cannot use any other timestamp
Last-access tracking is enabled automatically and requires no account-level setting
correct_answers: Creation, last modified, or last accessed can drive the age clock depending on the condition you choose
Enabling last-access tracking has its own setting you turn on at the account level

llm.prompt: Explain why the module frames lifecycle management as a shift from imperative to declarative data management, and what concrete operational problems this shift removes for a team like Meridian Parcel.
llm.reference_answer: The imperative approach means writing a nightly script that loops over blobs and decides which to move or delete, which is fragile: it requires compute to run and monitor, and an off-by-one bug walking a paginated listing of millions of objects can let data linger and run up the bill or get deleted too aggressively and trigger a compliance incident. The declarative lifecycle management policy instead describes the desired end state as JSON rules attached to the storage account, for example that blobs under proof-of-delivery/ unmodified for 30 days belong in cool, archive after 365 days, and delete after 2,555 days. The platform then enforces those rules continuously on its own evaluation schedule. This removes the need to run or monitor a job, eliminates the hand-rolled cleanup code where data is mishandled, and lets storage tier and expire itself correctly without custom compute.

llm.prompt: Walk through how you would safely apply Meridian's photo retention policy with the Azure CLI, including the ordering of steps and what each step accomplishes.
llm.reference_answer: First, author the policy JSON with a rule whose filter scopes it to blobTypes blockBlob and prefixMatch proof-of-delivery/, and whose baseBlob actions form an aging ladder keyed to days since modification: tierToCool after 30 days, tierToArchive after 365 days, and delete after 2,555 days. Before applying it, run az storage account blob-service-properties update with --enable-delete-retention true, --delete-retention-days 14, and --enable-versioning true, so that 14-day soft delete and versioning are on and any mistaken delete is recoverable. Only then run az storage account management-policy create with --policy @pod-lifecycle.json to attach the lifecycle policy to the account. In production you would commit the JSON to source control and apply it through a pipeline, and you should expect the first evaluation pass within roughly a day rather than immediately.

llm.prompt: Compare blob soft delete and blob versioning as protection features for automated lifecycle deletion, and explain how each contributes to making automated deletion safe.
llm.reference_answer: Both are account-level features that turn a mistaken automated deletion from a data-loss event into a quick restore. Blob soft delete retains deleted blobs for a configurable retention period (14 days in the walkthrough), so an accidental delete by a person or by a policy rule can be undone within the window before the data is purged for good. Blob versioning automatically keeps a previous version whenever a blob is overwritten or deleted, so you can restore the prior state. Together they ensure that if a wrong prefix or off-by-one day threshold purges live data, the affected blobs are recoverable, which is why the module insists you turn both on before letting any lifecycle delete action run. They are distinct from an immutability policy, which prevents deletion outright for compliance rather than enabling recovery after the fact.
